lessons.title: What Is a Package Manager?
lessons.content:
**Hook.** On your phone you install apps from an app store: you tap once, and the right program is downloaded and set up for you. On Linux, the everyday way to install software is almost the same idea, but you run it from the command line. The tool that does this is called a **package manager** — think of it as a built-in app store you type commands to.

**Build.** A *package* is just a program bundled up so it is ready to install (its files plus a label saying its name and version). The package manager downloads packages from trusted online collections called **repositories** (or *repos* for short) — central libraries of software that the makers of your Linux system keep up to date. So the package manager fetches packages from repositories, installs them, and can update or remove them later, all for you.

Different Linux *families* ship different package managers, but the idea is identical. On **Debian and Ubuntu** (a very common beginner choice) the command is `apt`. Here are the everyday actions:

```bash
sudo apt update              # refresh the list of what's available
sudo apt install firefox     # download and install a program
sudo apt remove firefox      # uninstall a program
apt search editor            # find packages matching a word
```

`sudo` means "run this as the administrator," which installing software requires. `apt update` does not upgrade your programs — it just refreshes the *catalog* so `apt` knows what exists and which versions. Installing then pulls the program from a repository.

**Recap.** A package manager is a command-line app store. It pulls software (packages) from trusted online repositories and handles installing, updating, and removing it. On Ubuntu that tool is `apt`, and the four actions you will use constantly are update, install, remove, and search.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is a package manager?
choices: A text editor for writing programs
A command-line tool that installs, updates, and removes software from trusted online repositories
A file that contains a single program's source code
A web browser for downloading files by hand
items.explanation: A package manager is like an app store you run from the command line: it fetches software from repositories and installs, updates, or removes it for you. It is not an editor, a browser, or a single program's source.

items.prompt: What is a 'repository' in the context of installing Linux software?
choices: A folder of your personal documents
A trusted online collection of packages that the package manager downloads from
The name of the install command on Ubuntu
A backup copy of your hard drive
items.explanation: A repository (repo) is a central, trusted online library of software the package manager pulls from. It is not your documents, a backup, or a command name.

items.prompt: You are on Ubuntu and want to install software the recommended way. Which command should you use?
choices: sudo apt install <program>
download a random .exe from a website and double-click it
make install
git clone the program
items.explanation: On Ubuntu (a Debian-family system) the standard, safe way to install software is `sudo apt install <program>`, which pulls a vetted package from the repositories. `.exe` files are for Windows, `make install` builds from source (advanced), and `git clone` only copies code without installing it.

items.prompt: What does 'sudo' at the start of 'sudo apt install firefox' mean?
choices: Run the command silently with no output
Run the command as the administrator (with the privileges needed to install software)
Skip the download and use a cached copy
Search instead of install
items.explanation: `sudo` runs the command with administrator privileges, which is required to install system software. It does not silence output, skip downloads, or change install into search.

items.prompt: What does 'sudo apt update' actually do?
choices: Upgrades all your installed programs to the newest version
Refreshes the local catalog so apt knows what packages and versions are available
Deletes old packages to free disk space
Installs the most popular package automatically
items.explanation: `apt update` only refreshes the list (catalog) of available packages and versions from the repositories. Actually upgrading installed programs is a separate command (`apt upgrade`). It does not delete or auto-install anything.

items.prompt: Which command removes (uninstalls) a program on Ubuntu?
choices: sudo apt search firefox
sudo apt update firefox
sudo apt remove firefox
sudo apt list firefox
items.explanation: `sudo apt remove firefox` uninstalls the program. `search` finds packages by keyword, `update` refreshes the catalog, and `list` shows package info.

items.prompt: You don't know the exact package name for a text editor. Which command helps you find it?
choices: apt search editor
apt remove editor
apt update editor
sudo editor install
items.explanation: `apt search editor` lists packages whose name or description matches the word 'editor', helping you discover the right name. The others remove, refresh the catalog, or are not valid commands.

items.prompt: Why is installing through the package manager usually safer than downloading software from a random website?
choices: Because the package manager makes downloads faster
Because packages come from trusted repositories that are vetted and checked, instead of unknown sources
Because it installs software without using any disk space
Because websites cannot host Linux software at all
items.explanation: Repositories are curated and trusted sources, so packages are far less likely to be tampered with than a random download. Speed and disk usage are not the point, and websites certainly can host Linux software (it is just riskier).

items.prompt: A friend says 'just run apt update and your apps will be the newest versions.' Are they right?
choices: Yes, apt update upgrades every installed app
No, apt update only refreshes the catalog; upgrading installed apps is a separate step
Yes, but only for apps you installed today
No, apt update actually removes apps
items.explanation: This is a common beginner mix-up. `apt update` refreshes the list of what is available; it does not change your installed programs. Upgrading them is done separately (with `apt upgrade`).

lessons.title: Dependencies and the Two Main Families
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Imagine asking a librarian for one book, and they hand you that book plus every other book it refers to, so you actually have everything you need to read it. A package manager does the same for software: when you install one program, it quietly finds and installs the *other* programs that program relies on.

**Build.** Most software is built on top of smaller shared pieces of code. The extra programs a piece of software needs in order to run are called its **dependencies**. For example, a photo editor might depend on an image-handling library and a menu toolkit, and those might in turn depend on something else. Working out this whole chain and installing it in the right order is called **dependency resolution**, and the package manager does it for you automatically. This is the single biggest reason package managers exist: you ask for one thing, and you get a working result instead of a pile of "missing file" errors.

```bash
sudo apt install gimp     # also pulls in everything gimp needs
```

Linux comes in **families**, and the two you will meet most are:

- **Debian / Ubuntu** family: the command is `apt`, and packages are files ending in **`.deb`**.
- **Fedora / RHEL** family: the command is `dnf`, and packages are files ending in **`.rpm`**.

The ideas are identical; mostly only the words change:

```bash
# Debian / Ubuntu        # Fedora / RHEL
sudo apt install pkg     sudo dnf install pkg
sudo apt remove pkg      sudo dnf remove pkg
sudo apt update          sudo dnf check-update
```

Both `apt` and `dnf` resolve dependencies automatically. A `.deb` and a `.rpm` are just two file formats for the same concept: a bundled, installable program with a name, a version, and a list of its dependencies inside.

**Recap.** Dependencies are the other programs your software needs; the package manager resolves and installs them for you. The two big families are Debian/Ubuntu (`apt`, `.deb`) and Fedora/RHEL (`dnf`, `.rpm`) — same concept, different names.

items.prompt: What is a 'dependency'?
choices: A setting that makes a program run faster
Another program or library that your software needs in order to work
The website a package was downloaded from
A backup copy of a package
items.explanation: A dependency is something else (a library or program) that your software relies on to function. It is not a speed setting, a download source, or a backup.

items.prompt: When you run 'sudo apt install gimp', what does the package manager resolve automatically for you?
choices: The screen resolution of your monitor
All the dependencies gimp needs, downloading and installing them too
Your internet connection speed
Which folder to save your photos in
items.explanation: The package manager automatically resolves dependencies: it figures out every other program gimp needs and installs those as well, so gimp actually works. It has nothing to do with screen resolution, connection speed, or your save folders.

items.prompt: The process of working out the full chain of needed packages and installing them in the right order is called what?
choices: Dependency resolution
Disk formatting
Compression
Encryption
items.explanation: Figuring out and installing the whole chain of dependencies is called dependency resolution. Formatting, compression, and encryption are unrelated operations.

items.prompt: On the Debian/Ubuntu family, the install command and package file extension are:
choices: dnf and .rpm
apt and .deb
pacman and .pkg
apt and .rpm
items.explanation: Debian/Ubuntu uses the `apt` command and `.deb` package files. `dnf`/`.rpm` belong to Fedora/RHEL; mixing them up (like apt with .rpm) is a common error.

items.prompt: On the Fedora/RHEL family, which command installs software?
choices: apt install
dnf install
deb install
rpm-get install
items.explanation: Fedora/RHEL uses `dnf install`. `apt` is the Debian/Ubuntu command, and the other two are not real commands.

items.prompt: How are a .deb file and a .rpm file related?
choices: They are completely different things with nothing in common
They are two file formats for the same idea: a bundled, installable program with its name, version, and dependency list
A .deb is software but a .rpm is only documentation
A .rpm is older and can no longer be installed
items.explanation: Both are package files: a program bundled with its metadata (name, version, dependencies). They are just the formats used by different families. Neither is mere documentation, and both are still in active use.

items.prompt: You learned to install software with apt on Ubuntu. You now sit at a Fedora machine. What mostly changes?
choices: Everything is completely different and nothing transfers
Mostly just the command name: you use dnf instead of apt for the same kinds of actions
You can no longer install software at all
You must build every program from source
items.explanation: The concepts (install, remove, update, dependencies, repositories) are the same across families; mostly the command name changes from apt to dnf. You do not lose the ability to install or have to build from source.

items.prompt: Two programs you install both need the same helper library. What does a good package manager do?
choices: Refuses to install the second program
Installs the shared library once and lets both programs use it
Installs a separate full copy of the library for each, always
Asks you to compile the library yourself
items.explanation: Traditional package managers share common dependencies: the helper library is installed once and reused by both programs (see the shared box at the bottom of the dependency tree). It does not refuse the install or force you to compile anything.

items.prompt: Why does automatic dependency resolution matter so much to a beginner?
choices: It makes your screen brighter
Without it, you would have to find and install each needed piece by hand and would hit 'missing file' errors
It is only useful for servers, not desktops
It prevents you from ever updating software
items.explanation: Dependency resolution saves you from manually hunting down every required piece (and the cascade of errors that follows when one is missing). It applies everywhere, helps with updates, and has nothing to do with screen brightness.

lessons.title: Trust, Version Pinning, and Building From Source
lessons.content:
**Hook.** If software is being downloaded from the internet and installed with administrator powers, one question matters a lot: how does your computer know the package is genuine and was not swapped out by an attacker? The answer is a digital seal, and understanding it makes you a safer Linux user.

**Build — trust through signing.** Every official repository signs its packages with a secret key, producing a **GPG signature** (think of it as an unforgeable wax seal; GPG just stands for the encryption tool that makes it). Your computer keeps the matching **public key** — the part that can *check* a seal but not create one. Before installing, the package manager verifies the seal against that trusted key. If the seal matches, the package is genuine; if anything was altered, the check fails and the package is refused. The crucial idea: **you trust a key, not a server.** Even a hacked download mirror cannot forge a valid seal without the secret key, so it cannot sneak a tampered package onto your system.

**Build — version pinning.** Normally you want the newest version, but sometimes you need to *freeze* a package at a known-good version (for example, a newer release broke something). Holding a package back is called **version pinning** (or a *hold*):

```bash
sudo apt-mark hold nginx     # keep nginx at its current version
sudo apt-mark unhold nginx   # allow it to upgrade again
```

The catch: a held package also stops getting *security* fixes, so a forgotten hold quietly becomes a risk. Pin deliberately, and review your holds.

**Build — which package owns a file, and building from source.** The package manager keeps a record of what it installed, so you can ask which package a file belongs to:

```bash
dpkg -S /usr/bin/curl     # Debian/Ubuntu: which package owns this file?
rpm -qf /usr/bin/curl     # Fedora/RHEL: the same question
```

Occasionally no package exists and you must **build from source** — turn the original code into a program yourself, classically `./configure && make && sudo make install`. The downside is that this just copies files in with *no record kept*, so there is no clean uninstall and no dependency tracking. That headache is exactly *why packaging exists*: to track everything for you.

**Recap.** Packages are signed, and you trust the key, not the server, which is why a bad mirror cannot tamper with installs. You can pin a package to freeze its version (but it then misses security fixes), ask which package owns a file, and build from source when needed, accepting that hand-built software is untracked.

items.prompt: What is a GPG signature on a package, in plain terms?
choices: A password you must type to install it
An unforgeable digital seal that proves the package is genuine and unaltered
The file size of the package
A list of the package's dependencies
items.explanation: A GPG signature is like a tamper-proof wax seal: it lets your computer confirm the package really came from the trusted source and was not changed. It is not a password, a size, or a dependency list.

items.prompt: In package signing, what does your computer use to CHECK a package's seal?
choices: The repository's secret (private) key
A public key it already trusts, which can verify a seal but not create one
Your login password
The package's file name
items.explanation: Your computer holds the public key, which can verify a signature but cannot forge one. The secret/private key stays with the repository. Your password and the file name play no part in the check.

items.prompt: Why can a hacked download mirror NOT slip a tampered package onto a correctly set up system?
choices: Because the mirror lacks the secret signing key, so it cannot produce a valid seal that matches the trusted public key
Because mirrors are never connected to the internet
Because the package manager refuses to use mirrors at all
Because tampered packages are automatically larger and get rejected by size
items.explanation: Trust rests on the key, not the server. Without the secret key the mirror cannot forge a valid signature, so a tampered package fails the check. Mirrors are used and are online; size is not what is verified.

items.prompt: The phrase 'you trust a key, not a server' means:
choices: You should only download from one specific server forever
Trust is based on a cryptographic key, so any server (even an untrusted mirror) is fine as long as the signature checks out
Servers are more trustworthy than keys
You must enter a key every time you install
items.explanation: Because the signature proves authenticity, the package can come from any mirror and still be trusted once the seal verifies against your key. It does not mean sticking to one server or typing a key each time.

items.prompt: What does 'version pinning' (a hold) do?
choices: Deletes all old versions of a package
Freezes a package at its current version so it will not upgrade
Forces a package to always use the newest version
Pins the package icon to your desktop
items.explanation: Pinning/holding freezes a package at its current version (e.g. `apt-mark hold nginx`). It is the opposite of forcing the newest version, and it does not delete versions or touch your desktop.

items.prompt: What is the danger of leaving a package pinned (held) and forgetting about it?
choices: It uses too much disk space over time
It also stops receiving security updates, becoming a silent vulnerability
It will uninstall itself after a while
It makes the whole system refuse all updates
items.explanation: A held package is excluded from upgrades, including security fixes, so a forgotten hold quietly leaves a hole. It does not balloon disk usage, uninstall itself, or block updates to other packages.

items.prompt: A strange file '/usr/bin/widget' appears and you want to know which installed package put it there. On Ubuntu, which command answers that?
choices: dpkg -S /usr/bin/widget
apt update widget
make install widget
apt search /usr/bin/widget
items.explanation: `dpkg -S <file>` (on Fedora/RHEL it is `rpm -qf <file>`) maps a file back to the package that owns it. `apt update` refreshes the catalog, `make install` builds from source, and `apt search` matches keywords, not file paths.

items.prompt: What does 'building from source' mean?
choices: Downloading a ready-made package from a repository
Turning a program's original code into a runnable program yourself, often with ./configure && make && sudo make install
Copying a program from another computer
Renaming a package file
items.explanation: Building from source means compiling the original source code into a working program on your machine. Downloading a ready package, copying, or renaming are not the same thing.

items.prompt: Why is installing software with 'make install' usually worse than using a package?
choices: It always installs the wrong version
It copies files in with no record kept, so there is no clean uninstall and no dependency tracking
It requires an internet connection and packages do not
It can only install one program ever
items.explanation: `make install` just copies files into place with nothing tracking them, so removing it cleanly or managing its dependencies is hard. Solving exactly that bookkeeping problem is why package managers exist.

items.prompt: How is the package manager able to tell you which package owns a given file?
choices: It guesses based on the file name
It keeps a record (a database) of every file each package installed
It re-downloads the whole internet to check
It cannot; that information is lost after install
items.explanation: When it installs a package, the package manager records which files came from it, so it can later map any file back to its owner. It is not guessing, re-downloading, or losing the information.

lessons.title: Modern App Formats, Language Tools, and Staying Updated
lessons.content:
**Hook.** The classic system package manager (`apt`, `dnf`) is great, but it ties each program to your specific Linux version and its shared libraries. Newer formats take a different approach: bundle everything an app needs into one self-contained unit so it runs the same on any Linux. And separately, the programming world has its own installers. Knowing how these fit together keeps you out of a common beginner trap.

**Build — universal app formats.** These package an app together with its dependencies, so they do not depend on the host system's exact libraries:

- **Flatpak** — desktop apps from a store called Flathub; each app runs in a *sandbox* (a walled-off area limiting what it can touch).
- **Snap** — Canonical's version, similar idea, with automatic updates.
- **AppImage** — the simplest: one downloadable file you make executable and run, with *no install step* at all.

The trade-off: these bundle their own copies of libraries, so they use more disk space, but they are portable across distributions and isolated from the system.

**Build — language package managers and the tension.** Programming languages ship their *own* installers for code libraries: **pip** for Python, **npm** for Node.js (JavaScript), **cargo** for Rust. These are separate from the system package manager and the OS does not track them. The trap: running something like `sudo pip install` system-wide lets a language tool fight the system tool over the same files and can break things. The discipline is to keep language libraries in a per-project space (for Python this is a **virtual environment**, a `venv`) and let the system manager own the system. This is the *system-versus-language tension*.

**Build — staying updated.** Keeping software current is mostly about applying **security updates** promptly:

```bash
sudo apt upgrade     # install available updates (Debian/Ubuntu)
sudo dnf upgrade     # the same on Fedora/RHEL
```

On Ubuntu you can enable **unattended-upgrades** so security patches install automatically in the background — set-and-forget protection.

**Recap.** Flatpak, Snap, and AppImage bundle an app with its dependencies for portability and isolation, at the cost of size. Language tools (pip, npm, cargo) manage code libraries separately from the system — keep them project-local to avoid conflicts. And the most important habit is applying security updates, ideally automatically.

items.prompt: What do Flatpak, Snap, and AppImage have in common compared to a traditional .deb or .rpm?
choices: They contain no actual program, only settings
They bundle an app together with its dependencies so it runs across different Linux systems
They only work without an internet connection
They can only install command-line tools
items.explanation: All three bundle the app plus its dependencies, making them portable across distributions and independent of the host's exact libraries. They contain real programs (often graphical) and are not limited to offline or command-line use.

items.prompt: Which format is a single downloadable file that you make executable and run, with no install step?
choices: Flatpak
Snap
AppImage
apt
items.explanation: An AppImage is one self-contained file you can run directly after making it executable. Flatpak and Snap install through their own systems, and apt is the traditional package manager.

items.prompt: What is the main trade-off of bundled formats like Flatpak and Snap?
choices: They cannot be uninstalled
They use more disk space because each app carries its own copies of libraries
They only run on Windows
They never receive updates
items.explanation: Because each bundled app ships its own dependencies instead of sharing system libraries, it takes more disk space. They can be uninstalled, run on Linux, and do receive updates.

items.prompt: What is a 'sandbox' in the context of a Flatpak or Snap app?
choices: A folder for temporary files
A walled-off area that limits what the app can access on your system
The store you download apps from
A tool for building apps from source
items.explanation: A sandbox isolates the app, restricting what it can touch, which improves security. It is not a temp folder, a store, or a build tool.

items.prompt: pip, npm, and cargo are examples of what?
choices: System package managers that replace apt
Language package managers that install code libraries for Python, Node.js, and Rust
Universal app formats like Flatpak
Text editors
items.explanation: pip (Python), npm (Node.js/JavaScript), and cargo (Rust) install libraries for their programming languages. They are separate from the system manager, are not app-bundling formats, and are not editors.

items.prompt: Why can running 'sudo pip install' system-wide cause problems?
choices: It is too slow to finish
A language tool ends up fighting the system package manager over the same files, which can break the system
pip cannot install anything at all
It deletes your home folder
items.explanation: Installing language libraries system-wide lets pip and the OS package manager clash over the same files (the system-versus-language tension), risking breakage. It is not about speed, and it does not disable pip or erase your home folder.

items.prompt: What is the recommended way to install Python libraries for a single project without disturbing the system?
choices: Use sudo pip install for everything
Use a virtual environment (venv) so the libraries stay project-local
Replace the system Python with a new one
Install them with apt only, never pip
items.explanation: A virtual environment keeps a project's Python libraries isolated from the system Python, avoiding conflicts. Installing system-wide with sudo or replacing the system Python is exactly what causes breakage.

items.prompt: On Ubuntu, which command installs available updates for your installed software?
choices: sudo apt search
sudo apt upgrade
sudo apt remove
sudo apt hold
items.explanation: `sudo apt upgrade` installs the available updates (on Fedora/RHEL it is `dnf upgrade`). `search` finds packages, `remove` uninstalls, and `hold`/`apt-mark hold` freezes a version.

items.prompt: On Debian/Ubuntu, what does 'unattended-upgrades' do?
choices: Freezes all packages so they never change
Automatically installs security updates in the background without manual action
Removes software you have not used recently
Disables the package manager
items.explanation: unattended-upgrades applies security (and optionally other) updates automatically, so your system stays patched without you remembering to run it. It does not freeze packages, remove unused software, or disable the package manager.

items.prompt: Why are security updates considered the most important update habit?
choices: They make programs look nicer
They fix known weaknesses that attackers could otherwise exploit
They are the only updates that exist
They free up disk space
items.explanation: Security updates patch known vulnerabilities, closing holes before they can be exploited, which is why applying them promptly matters most. They are not about appearance, are not the only kind of update, and are not primarily about disk space.
